case_name: Smt. Sushma Yadav & Ors. vs State Of U.P. & Another on 6 November, 2019
text: consent of counsel for the parties, the record of Criminal Misc. Case U/s 482 No. 4925 of 2019 is being summoned for its disposal. These cases have a chequered history although not a very heartening one arising out of matrimonial discord in between wife Smt. Sushma Yadav and the husband Santosh Kumar Yadav. The said two entered into a matrimonial alliance and were blessed with a son, who is said to be aged about 7 years at present. However, on account of the litigations in between the parties, the total number of cases pending against each other in various courts exceed 23 in number. When the matter was being argued today, an effort was made for reconciliation, in view of the genesis of the litigation being a matrimonial discord in between the husband and the wife and also in the quest for ensuring the welfare of the child, who is caught in between web of cases launched against each other and the families. With the co-operations of the counsels, the Court could convince the parties to try and mutually settled the disputes amongst themselves. The matter was posted after lunch for permitting the parties to explore the possibility of settlement.

case_name: Smt. Sushma Yadav & Ors. vs State Of U.P. & Another on 6 November, 2019
text: caught in between web of cases launched against each other and the families. With the co-operations of the counsels, the Court could convince the parties to try and mutually settled the disputes amongst themselves. The matter was posted after lunch for permitting the parties to explore the possibility of settlement. When the matter was taken up after lunch, counsel for the parties have shown maturity, which is expected from the seniors at the Bar and have persuaded the parties for settlement of the disputes. Sri Salil Kumar Srivastava, learned counsel representing the wife and her relatives and friends in various litigations has offered that if the matter was finally settled in between the parties, he would have no objection. Similarly, Sri Kunwar Mukul Rakesh, learned Senior Advocate assisted by Sri Amrit Preet Singh, learned counsel appearing on behalf of opposite party-husband have also accepted that if all the matters are settled, there is no objection to the settling of the disputes that have arisen in between the parties. The parties file fresh proceedings for divorce by mutual consent under of the Hindu Marriage Act, which may be decided expeditiously within a time bound period as may be fixed by this Court. He argues that the filing of the divorce petition should be indicated in the order itself as well as the waiver of cooling off period be provided, in view of the fact that there is irretrievable breakdown of marriage and as the Apex Court in ; (2017) 8 SCC 746. 2. Sri Srivastava agrees that his client shall not insist on any alimony either permanent or otherwise as a condition for grant of divorce by mutual consent. 3. It is also agreed by Sri Srivastava that the custody of child Master Subham Yadav @ Vansh shall remain with the father, who is the natural guardian and the mother shall not claim the custody of child. Counsel for the opposite parties appearing on behalf of husband and the related parties agreed to all the three conditions and further requests that

case_name: Smt. Sushma Yadav & Ors. vs State Of U.P. & Another on 6 November, 2019
text: child Master Subham Yadav @ Vansh shall remain with the father, who is the natural guardian and the mother shall not claim the custody of child. Counsel for the opposite parties appearing on behalf of husband and the related parties agreed to all the three conditions and further requests that in the discretion of the Court all the compliances be made time bound and the proceedings pending in various Courts be quashed in exercise of powers under Cr.P.C., read with , of the Constitution of India only to give quietus to the litigations, which is pending in between the parties. It is also agreed in between the parties that no claim in respect of or arising out of the matrimonial alliance shall be claimed by either of the parties against each other including the benefits accruing owing to Government service, in which the opposite party-husband is said to have been in vocation at present. The parties agreed that they shall not raise any claim against each other in respect of or arising out of the matrimonial alliance which otherwise they would have been entitled to under either the Statute or the Rules applicable with regard to their respective vocations. In

case_name: Smt. Sushma Yadav & Ors. vs State Of U.P. & Another on 6 November, 2019
text: in vocation at present. In view of the fact that the parties have mutually come up with the proposal to which both the parties are agreeable this Court in the quest of giving quietus to the litigations as well as keeping in view the larger interest of the welfare of the child, this Court provides as under:- The parties, namely, Smt. Sushma Yadav and Santosh Kumar Yadav shall jointly file an petition for divorce on the ground of mutual consent under of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 without levelling any allegations against each other of any nature except on the ground that the parties are unable to stay with each other and, therefore, are seeking divorce by mutual consent. The said petition shall be filed before the Family Court, Lucknow by 2nd December, 2019. Both the parties shall present themselves before the Family Court on 2nd December, 2019 at 11:00 A.M. for filing the said

case_name: Smt. Sushma Yadav & Ors. vs State Of U.P. & Another on 6 November, 2019
text: unable to stay with each other and, therefore, are seeking divorce by mutual consent. The said petition shall be filed before the Family Court, Lucknow by 2nd December, 2019. Both the parties shall present themselves before the Family Court on 2nd December, 2019 at 11:00 A.M. for filing the said petition and for making the requisite documents available for filing it before the Family Court. The parties shall also file an application, simultaneously on the date of filing of the divorce petition, seeking waiver of cooling off period which the Family Court, Lucknow directed to decide and grant and thereafter proceed to pass an order on the application seeking divorce by mutual consent under of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955. The petition for divorce shall specifically contain an affidavit on behalf of the wife that she will not claim any maintenance or alimony arising out of matrimonial alliance, in which they had entered into. She will specifically discloses in her petition that she will not want custody of child, Master Subham Yadav @ Vansh. On the filing of the said petition, the Family Court, as directed, shall pass decree of divorce by mutual consent within a period not exceeding 15

case_name: Smt. Sushma Yadav & Ors. vs State Of U.P. & Another on 6 November, 2019
text: they had entered into. She will specifically discloses in her petition that she will not want custody of child, Master Subham Yadav @ Vansh. On the filing of the said petition, the Family Court, as directed, shall pass decree of divorce by mutual consent within a period not exceeding 15 days from the date of filing of the petition. The Family Court is further directed to send a copy of this order to the Senior Registrar of this Court for it being kept on the record within a further period of 15 days from the date of passing of the decree of divorce by mutual consent. case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025 Author: Bench: , $~J- * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI Judgment reserved on: 19.09.2025 Judgment delivered on: 17.12.2025 + MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 SHIKSHA KUMARI .....Appellant Through: Mr. Rajshekhar Rao, Sr. Advocate (Amicus-Curiae) with Ms. Aashna Chawla, Mr. Ajay Sabharwal, Mr. Wamic Wasim Nargal and Mr. Zahid Laiq Ahmed, Advocates. versus SANTOSH KUMAR .....Respondent Through: Mr. Saurabh Kansal, Advocate with Mr. Raghav Vij, Mr. Suraj Kumar, Ms. Ritul Sharma, and Mr. Pratham Malik, Advocates. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NAVIN CHAWLA HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ANUP JAIRAM BHAMBHANI HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE RENU BHATNAGAR J U D G M E N T ANUP JAIRAM BHAMBHANI, J. Is a court mandated to stall divorce by mutual consent, thrusting unwilling parties - not into marital bliss, but into a matrimonial abyss? The present reference arises from judgment dated 22.04.2025 rendered by a Division Bench of this court in MAT.APP. (F.C.) No. 111/2025, concerning the timeline prescribed for the presentation of a Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 1 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 petition for divorce by mutual consent under of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (―HMA‖). Having regard to the

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: of this court in MAT.APP. (F.C.) No. 111/2025, concerning the timeline prescribed for the presentation of a Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 1 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 petition for divorce by mutual consent under of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (―HMA‖). Having regard to the view taken by an earlier Division Bench of this court in , 1 the Division Bench in the present matter has observed that the interpretation adopted in Sankalp Singh may warrant reconsideration. 2. In light of the divergent opinion that the Division Bench was inclined to express in its judgment dated 22.04.2025, it has formulated specific questions of law and requested Hon'ble the Chief Justice to place the matter before a Full Bench of this court for authoritative determination. The legal questions referred to the Full Bench read as follows: ―(a) Whether a petition under of the Hindu Marriage Act can be filed by the parties before completing the period of separation of one year?; (b) If the answer to the above question is in the affirmative, whether the period of six months between the presentation of the First Motion under of the Hindu Marriage Act and the Second Motion

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: can be filed by the parties before completing the period of separation of one year?; (b) If the answer to the above question is in the affirmative, whether the period of six months between the presentation of the First Motion under of the Hindu Marriage Act and the Second Motion under of the Hindu Marriage Act, can be waived off by the learned Court even though the parties have not been living separately for more than one year on the date when such waiver is prayed for?‖ 3. To get the context of the issue involved, the text of of the HMA may be noticed : 13B. Divorce by mutual consent.--(1) Subject to the provisions of this Act a petition for dissolution of marriage by a decree of divorce may be presented to the district court by both the parties to a marriage together, whether such marriage was solemnized before or after the commencement of the (68 of 1976), on the ground that they have 1 2013 SCC OnLine Del 855 Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 2 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 been living separately for a period of one year or more, that they have

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: or after the commencement of the (68 of 1976), on the ground that they have 1 2013 SCC OnLine Del 855 Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 2 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 been living separately for a period of one year or more, that they have not been able to live together and that they have mutually agreed that the marriage should be dissolved. (2) On the motion of both the parties made not earlier than six months after the date of the presentation of the petition referred to in sub-section (1) and not later than eighteen months after the said date, if the petition is not withdrawn in the meantime, the court shall, on being satisfied, after hearing the parties and after making such inquiry as it thinks fit, that a marriage has been solemnized and that the averments in the petition are true, pass a decree of divorce declaring the marriage to be dissolved with effect from the date of the decree. (emphasis supplied) 4. Also relevant for purposes of the present proceedings is of the HMA, which reads as follows : 14. No petition for divorce to be presented within one year of

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: a decree of divorce declaring the marriage to be dissolved with effect from the date of the decree. (emphasis supplied) 4. Also relevant for purposes of the present proceedings is of the HMA, which reads as follows : 14. No petition for divorce to be presented within one year of marriage.--(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, it shall not be competent for any court to entertain any petition for dissolution of a marriage by a decree of divorce, unless at the date of the presentation of the petition one year has elapsed since the date of the marriage: Provided that the court may, upon application made to it in accordance with such rules as may be made by the High Court in that behalf, allow a petition to be presented before one year has elapsed since the date of the marriage on the ground that the case is one of exceptional hardship to the petitioner or of exceptional depravity on the part of the respondent, but if it appears to the court at the hearing of the petition that the petitioner obtained leave to present the petition by any misrepresentation or concealment of the nature of the case, the

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: of exceptional hardship to the petitioner or of exceptional depravity on the part of the respondent, but if it appears to the court at the hearing of the petition that the petitioner obtained leave to present the petition by any misrepresentation or concealment of the nature of the case, the court may, if it pronounces a decree, do so subject to the condition that the decree shall not have effect until after the expiry of one year from the date of the marriage or may dismiss the petition without prejudice to any petition which may be brought after expiration of the said one year upon the same or substantially the same facts as those alleged in support of the petition so dismissed. Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 3 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 (2) In disposing of any application under this section for leave to present a petition for divorce before the expiration of one year from the date of the marriage, the court shall have regard to the interests of any children of the marriage and to the question whether there is a reasonable probability of a reconciliation between the parties before the expiration of

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: a petition for divorce before the expiration of one year from the date of the marriage, the court shall have regard to the interests of any children of the marriage and to the question whether there is a reasonable probability of a reconciliation between the parties before the expiration of the said one year. (emphasis supplied) 5. Since this reference arises from the decision of a Division Bench of this court in Sankalp Singh, the relevant extract of the judgment must be noticed : ―30. A more liberal construction can envisage the application of the proviso to of the said Act without compromising on the essential ingredients of of the said Act. This is possible by ensuring that none of the three essential ingredients are compromised. Thus, parties should have been living separately for one (1) year or more, that they have not been able to live together and have mutually agreed that the marriage should be dissolved. However, the dissolution of marriage has to take effect only after the hiatus period of six (6) to eighteen (18) months, on the second motion being filed. Thus, before such a decree of divorce is passed post second motion the period of

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: mutually agreed that the marriage should be dissolved. However, the dissolution of marriage has to take effect only after the hiatus period of six (6) to eighteen (18) months, on the second motion being filed. Thus, before such a decree of divorce is passed post second motion the period of one (1) year of separation ought to have elapsed but in order to present the first motion, the requirement of one (1) year separation would not apply provided it meets the parameters of proviso to of the said Act. This view would not compromise on the essential ingredients of any part of of the said Act and simultaneously respect the wisdom of the legislature which enacted of the said Act and incorporated it by insertion with sub-section (1) beginning with "Subject to the provisions of this Act" which would include Section 14. Not only that of the said Act itself begins with a "Notwithstanding" clause. This would, thus, be the harmonious construction of the provisions of the said Act which would enable to give meaning to all the relevant provisions of the said Act without compromising the ingredients of any. Such a course of action is possible especially because there

By way of amendment in the year 1976, the concept of divorce by mutual consent was introduced. However, contains a bar to divorce being granted before six months of time elapsing after filing of the divorce petition by mutual consent. The said period was laid down to enable the parties to have a rethink so that the court grants divorce by mutual consent only if there is no chance for reconciliation. ―17. The object of the provision is to enable the parties to dissolve a marriage by consent if the marriage has irretrievably broken down and to enable them to rehabilitate them as per available options. The object of the cooling-off period was to safeguard against a hurried decision if there was otherwise possibility of differences being reconciled. The object was not to perpetuate a purposeless marriage or to prolong the agony of the parties when there was no chance of reconciliation. Though every effort has to be made to save a marriage, if there are no chances of reunion and there are chances of fresh rehabilitation, the Court should not be powerless in enabling the parties to have a better option. ―18. If object of the enactment will be defeated by holding the same directory, it will be construed as mandatory, whereas if by holding it mandatory serious general inconvenience will be created to innocent persons without very much furthering the object of enactment, the same will be construed as directory." However, upon a scrutiny of the legal position as it stands subsequent to the aforesaid pronouncements, it becomes clear that the law has since evolved, and the jurisprudential landscape has undergone a substantial transformation. 22. To appreciate how the law has evolved in relation to the timelines set- out in of the HMA over a period of time, it would be necessary to summarise the most relevant verdicts of the Supreme Court on the point: 22.1. In 1991, in its decision in Sureshta Devi, the Supreme Court held that the requirement that parties must have been living

In 1997, in Ashok Hurra, a co-equal Bench of the Supreme Court did not overturn the decision in Sureshta Devi but observed that certain observations in Sureshta Devi appear to be ―very wide and may require reconsideration in an appropriate case‖. However, as noted above, the observation of the Supreme Court in Ashok Hurra was with reference to whether it is necessary for the consent of parties to continue till the decree of divorce is granted, and in that case, the second motion had come-up for consideration beyond the 18-month Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 18 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 period stipulated in of the HMA.14 Therefore, in Ashok Hurra the Supreme Court did not digress from the view taken in Sureshta Devi on the point whether the 01-year period

The decision in Ashok Hurra therefore has no application or relevance for purposes of the issues referred to this Full Bench. 22.3. In 2009, while deciding Anil Kumar Jain, a 02-Judge Bench of Supreme Court reaffirmed the principles laid-down in Sureshta Devi. 22.4. In 2017, in Amardeep Singh, the Supreme Court held that the ―court‖ - which would include the Family Court and the High Court - has the discretion to waive the 06-month statutory period prescribed under of the HMA. This observation of the Supreme Court is relevant, on point of principle, and would aid us in deciding whether the timeline prescribed in of the HMA is mandatory or discretionary, in particular context of Sankalp Singh having held that it is necessary for the 01-year separation period to have elapsed before a mutual consent divorce decree is granted. In fact waiver of the 01-year period under and waiver of the 06-month period under 13B(2), must be treated as two separate and distinct aspects; but waiver of one timeline would not preclude waiver of the other. 43. We have already noticed that in Amardeep Singh the Supreme Court has held that the 06-month cooling-off period as prescribed under of the HMA is not mandatory but directory, and has laid-down certain circumstances in which such period may be waived by the court. This principle was further expanded by the Supreme Court in Amit Kumar. Therefore, merely because, based on certain exceptional circumstances the court decides

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: period as prescribed under of the HMA is not mandatory but directory, and has laid-down certain circumstances in which such period may be waived by the court. This principle was further expanded by the Supreme Court in Amit Kumar. Therefore, merely because, based on certain exceptional circumstances the court decides to exercise its powers under the proviso to of the HMA to waive the period of 01-year before the first motion can be presented by the parties, that would not denude the court of its power to also waive the cooling-off period between the first motion and the second motion. 44. However, we must hasten to add that there can be certain cases where the court, in its discretion, decides that though it would entertain a petition before expiration of the 01-year period of separation, it would be appropriate to give more time to the parties to reconsider their decision; and may refuse to grant them exemption from adhering to the mandatory 06-month cooling-off period. Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 32 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 45. To put to rest any emotive argument against hasty dissolution of a marriage, we would only say that though

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: and may refuse to grant them exemption from adhering to the mandatory 06-month cooling-off period. Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 32 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 45. To put to rest any emotive argument against hasty dissolution of a marriage, we would only say that though a marriage is most certainly a solemn commitment, however once spouses mutually agree to end their union, the law must not interfere in their decisional autonomy. Since a marriage must necessarily be the result of free consent between adults, a divorce by mutual consent should also not be obstructed, thrusting unwilling parties - not into marital bliss, but into a matrimonial abyss. 46. In the passing, it may also be observed that when a petition for divorce is presented with consent of parties, who aver that they have been living separately for a period of 01-year or more, the only circumstance in which the period of separation can be verified is if the marriage itself is of less than 01-year vintage. In all other cases, the mere consensual assertion of the parties is sufficient to fulfil the requirements of of the HMA and cannot be second- guessed. In a sense

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: circumstance in which the period of separation can be verified is if the marriage itself is of less than 01-year vintage. In all other cases, the mere consensual assertion of the parties is sufficient to fulfil the requirements of of the HMA and cannot be second- guessed. In a sense therefore, verification of the 01-year separation period in is all but illusive. 47. In our opinion therefore, the view that the timeline stipulated in of the HMA is immutable, would render the opening words of of the HMA-viz., that the section is subject to the other provisions of the statute - nugatory. We must remind ourselves, that on first principles, a court must never presume that the Legislature has wasted its words or that any words used in a statute are superfluous or redundant.29 29 (2002) 1 SCC 193, para 11 Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 33 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 48. As a sequitur of the above discussion, we agree with the view taken by the Division Bench of this court in Sankalp Singh, insofar as it holds that a petition under of the HMA can be entertained even before parties have lived separately

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: Page 33 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 48. As a sequitur of the above discussion, we agree with the view taken by the Division Bench of this court in Sankalp Singh, insofar as it holds that a petition under of the HMA can be entertained even before parties have lived separately for a period of 01-year. 49. However, we differ from the view taken in Sankalp Singh when the Division Bench says that in order to satisfy the pre-requisite under of the HMA, if the first motion is entertained and allowed before the parties have lived separately for at least 01-year, the second motion must only be allowed and a divorce decree can only be granted after the period of 01-year of separation is complete. It must be understood that at the time Sankalp Singh was pronounced, viz., in 2013, it was impermissible to waive even the 06-month period under ; and the law that the 06-month period could be waived came by way of the Supreme Court ruling in Amardeep Singh only in 2017. In our reading of Sankalp Singh, it was in these circumstances that the Division Bench held that though the proviso to applies even to petitions under

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: and the law that the 06-month period could be waived came by way of the Supreme Court ruling in Amardeep Singh only in 2017. In our reading of Sankalp Singh, it was in these circumstances that the Division Bench held that though the proviso to applies even to petitions under , the first motion may be entertained and allowed; but the second motion can only be allowed and a decree of divorce granted after the 01-year of separation period under has elapsed. Considering the manner in which the law has progressed since 2013 however, we disagree with the view taken in Sankalp Singh on that aspect; and we hold that the second motion may be entertained and allowed and a divorce decree may also be granted even before parties have lived separately for a period of less that 01-year subject to: Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 34 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 (i) the court being satisfied that the circumstances envisaged in the proviso to of the HMA viz., of exceptional hardship or exceptional depravity, exist; and (ii) the court also testing the case on the anvil of the indicative considerations set-out in Pooja Gupta. The

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: Page 34 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 (i) the court being satisfied that the circumstances envisaged in the proviso to of the HMA viz., of exceptional hardship or exceptional depravity, exist; and (ii) the court also testing the case on the anvil of the indicative considerations set-out in Pooja Gupta. The contrary view taken in Sankalp Singh, is, to that extent, set- aside. 50. We may further explain the reasons for our disagreement with Sankalp Singh. As narrated above, the rationalé for the Division Bench holding that the second motion can be presented but the ―decree of divorce would only be granted once the period of one (1) year has elapsed from the separation‖30 was to fulfil the pre-requisite set-out in of the HMA. However, we are of the view that once the court is satisfied that parties have made-out a case on the touchstone of what we have observed above; and the court decides to entertain the first motion before expiry of the 01-year separation period; and the parties have also made-out a case for waiver of the 06- month cooling-off period for presenting the second motion; and the court is also convinced that divorce should be granted on mutual

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: and the court decides to entertain the first motion before expiry of the 01-year separation period; and the parties have also made-out a case for waiver of the 06- month cooling-off period for presenting the second motion; and the court is also convinced that divorce should be granted on mutual consent, we see no justification whatsoever for the court to withhold the passing of the divorce decree till the 01-year separation period is completed. 51. As a matter of fact, a closer reading of the proviso to of the HMA would show that the option of deferring the effect of a 30 Sankalp Singh, para 30 Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 35 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 divorce decree was contemplated by the Legislature only in certain circumstances. The proviso to may be noticed: 14. No petition for divorce to be presented within one year of marriage.- (1) Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, it shall not be competent for any court to entertain any petition for dissolution of a marriage by a decree of divorce, unless at the date of the presentation of the petition one year has elapsed since the date of the marriage:

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: marriage.- (1) Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, it shall not be competent for any court to entertain any petition for dissolution of a marriage by a decree of divorce, unless at the date of the presentation of the petition one year has elapsed since the date of the marriage: Provided that the court may, upon application made to it in accordance with such rules as may be made by the High Court in that behalf, allow a petition to be presented before one year has elapsed since the date of the marriage on the ground that the case is one of exceptional hardship to the petitioner or of exceptional depravity on the part of the respondent, but if it appears to the court at the hearing of the petition that the petitioner obtained leave to present the petition by any misrepresentation or concealment of the nature of the case, the court may, if it pronounces a decree, do so subject to the condition that the decree shall not have effect until after the expiry of one year from the date of the marriage or may dismiss the petition without prejudice to any petition which may be brought after expiration

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: the court may, if it pronounces a decree, do so subject to the condition that the decree shall not have effect until after the expiry of one year from the date of the marriage or may dismiss the petition without prejudice to any petition which may be brought after expiration of the said one year upon the same or substantially the same facts as those alleged in support of the petition so dismissed. (emphasis supplied) 52. It is clear therefore, that the Legislature has not mandated that if a court has decided to grant a divorce decree, it must hold that the decree shall not have effect until after expiry of a period of 01 year from the date of marriage. Instead the Legislature has used the word ‗may', to say that ―...... if it appears to the court at the hearing of the petition that the petitioner obtained leave to present the petition by any misrepresentation or concealment of the nature of the case, the court may, if it pronounces a decree, do so subject to the condition Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 36 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 that the decree shall not have

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: to present the petition by any misrepresentation or concealment of the nature of the case, the court may, if it pronounces a decree, do so subject to the condition Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 36 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 that the decree shall not have effect until after the expiry of one year from the date of the marriage.....‖. The provision therefore contemplates, that a decree passed may be made effective from a later date only if the court finds that the petitioner has obtained leave to present the first motion by misrepresentation or concealment. It is not the purport of the proviso to that every decree of divorce obtained prior to the expiration of the period of 01 year must be made effective from a later date, as appears to have been erroneously construed or implied in Sankalp Singh. 53. In fact, where the court finds that there was no misrepresentation or concealment in obtaining the leave to file the first motion before expiry of the 01-year separation period, holding the divorce decree in abeyance till a later date would amount to the court adopting a conflicted position, inasmuch as, despite being satisfied that

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: court finds that there was no misrepresentation or concealment in obtaining the leave to file the first motion before expiry of the 01-year separation period, holding the divorce decree in abeyance till a later date would amount to the court adopting a conflicted position, inasmuch as, despite being satisfied that the exceptional circumstances contemplated in the proviso of exist, the court would yet keep two unwilling persons tied in matrimony. 54. It may be necessary to articulate, that even where spouses file for divorce by mutual consent, their wish to part-ways would always be rooted in some basis or reason, though they may have chosen to adopt the consensual route for divorce. In such circumstances, it would be wholly undesirable to keep spouses embroiled in a bad marriage, instead of releasing them from the matrimonial bond. It hardly needs enunciation that such a situation would inflict undue psychological and emotional distress on one or both spouses, and that in itself may qualify as exceptional hardship on them. Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 37 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 55. In certain cases, delaying the obliteration of the matrimonial bond may lead to one or the other

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: distress on one or both spouses, and that in itself may qualify as exceptional hardship on them. Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 37 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 55. In certain cases, delaying the obliteration of the matrimonial bond may lead to one or the other spouse being unable to form a meaningful relationship with someone else, which would affect their future prospects irreparably, including prospects of remarriage and social integration. In the face of such a situation, the argument opposing waiver of the statutory period, even though envisaged by the Legislature as a period for reconciliation, would pale into insignificance since all relationships cannot be mended. 56. Though there is no doubt that the sanctity, stability, and solemnity of the institution of marriage holds social and cultural significance in our society, however where the spouses are ad-idem that their marriage must be ended, efforts to save a broken marriage would give primacy to outward social pretense at the cost of autonomy and dignity of the affected couple. CONCLUSIONS 57. We may summarise our conclusions in response to the questions posed, as follows : 57.1. The statutory period of 01-year prescribed under of the HMA as

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: save a broken marriage would give primacy to outward social pretense at the cost of autonomy and dignity of the affected couple. CONCLUSIONS 57. We may summarise our conclusions in response to the questions posed, as follows : 57.1. The statutory period of 01-year prescribed under of the HMA as a pre-requisite for presenting the first motion, can be waived, by applying the proviso to of the HMA; 57.2. The waiver of the 01-year separation period under of the HMA does not preclude waiver of the 06-month cooling-off period for filing the second motion under ; and waiver of the 01-year period under , Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 38 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 and the 06-month period under , are to be considered independently of each other; 57.3. Where the court is satisfied that the 01-year period under and the 06-month period under of the HMA deserve to be waived, the court is not legally mandated to defer the date from which the divorce decree would take effect, and such decree may be made effective forthwith; 57.4. Such waiver is not to be granted merely for the asking but only upon the court being

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: the HMA deserve to be waived, the court is not legally mandated to defer the date from which the divorce decree would take effect, and such decree may be made effective forthwith; 57.4. Such waiver is not to be granted merely for the asking but only upon the court being satisfied that circumstances of ―exceptional hardship to the petitioner‖ and/or ―exceptional depravity on the part of the respondent‖ exist, while also testing the case on the anvil of the considerations set-out in Pooja Gupta; 57.5. Waiver, as above, can be granted both by the Family Court as well as the High Court; and 57.6. As contemplated in the proviso to of the HMA, where a court finds that the waiver of the 01-year period under has been obtained by misrepresentation or concealment, the court may defer the date on which the divorce would take effect, as may be considered appropriate; or may dismiss the divorce petition, at whichever stage it is pending, without prejudice to the right of the parties to present a fresh petition under of the HMA after expiration of the 01-year period, on the same or substantially the same facts as may have been pleaded in the

case_name: Shiksha Kumari vs Santosh Kumar on 17 December, 2025
text: or may dismiss the divorce petition, at whichever stage it is pending, without prejudice to the right of the parties to present a fresh petition under of the HMA after expiration of the 01-year period, on the same or substantially the same facts as may have been pleaded in the petition so dismissed. Signature Not Verified Signed By:ANJALI KAUSHIK Signing MAT.APP.(F.C.) 111/2025 Page 39 of 40 Date:17.12.2025 14:55 58. The reference is answered accordingly. 59. We would conclude by expressing our sincere gratitude to Mr. Rajshekhar Rao, learned senior counsel and Amicus-Curiae, ably assisted by Ms. Aashna Chawla, Mr. Ajay Sabharwal, Mr. Wamic Wasim Nargal and Mr. Zahid Laiq Ahmed, Advocates; and Mr. Saurabh Kansal, Advocate, all of whom have rendered invaluable assistance in the matter. It appears that despite the guiding principles in the case of Amardeep Singh, consistently followed by this Court in the case of Prateek Kutte and Sneha Garg, it appears that the same are not being followed in the Family Courts. A similar grievance was raised in the earlier petition in the case of Sneha Garg arising from the petition filed in the Family Court, Pune. Hence, to address the grievances raised by the learned counsel for the petitioners, I find it appropriate to call for reports from the learned Principal

case_name: Ayushi Doshi vs Nil on 17 October, 2024
text: A similar grievance was raised in the earlier petition in the case of Sneha Garg arising from the petition filed in the Family Court, Pune. Hence, to address the grievances raised by the learned counsel for the petitioners, I find it appropriate to call for reports from the learned Principal Judges of the Family Courts at Mumbai and Pune so that the reports can be placed before the Hon'ble the Chief Justice. Divorce by mutual consent.-(1) Subject to the provisions of this Act a petition for dissolution of marriage by a decree of divorce may be presented to the district court by both the parties to a marriage together, whether such marriage was solemnized before or after the commencement of the (68 of 1976), on the ground that they have been living separately for a period of one year or more, that they have not been able to live together and that they have mutually agreed that the marriage should be dissolved. (2) On the motion of both the parties made earlier than six months after the date of the presentation of the petition referred to in sub-section (1) and not later than eighteen months after the said date, if the petition is not withdrawn in the meantime, the Court shall, on being satisfied, after hearing the parties and after making such inquiry as it thinks fit, that a marriage has been solemnized and that the averments in the petition are true, pass a decree of divorce declaring the marriage to be dissolved with effect from the date of the decree." 20. Sub- Section (2) of Section 13B provides for a period of six months to enable the parties to withdraw from the petition. Sub-Section (2) further clarifies that if the petition is not withdrawn within six months and in the meantime, the Court shall on being satisfied after hearing the parties and after making such enquiry that the marriage has been solemnized and the averments in the petition are true, shall pass a decree of divorce declaring the marriage to be dissolved. The commonly used words "second motion" is nothing but a stage for actually passing the order as contemplated under sub-section (2) of Section 13B of the said Act. No.02 of 2022) seeking waiver/relaxation of cooling-off period of six months under of the Act of 1955 has been jointly filed by both the parties. In this context, the text of of the Act of 1955 would be relevant here and quoted below : "13-B. Divorce by mutual consent.-- (1) Subject to the provisions of this Act a petition for dissolution of marriage by a decree of divorce may be presented to the district court by both the parties to a marriage together, whether such marriage was solemnised before or after the commencement of the , on the ground that they have been living separately for a period of one year or more, that they have not been able to live together and

case_name: Sanjay Kumar Soni vs Smt. Geetanjali Chouhan on 28 September, 2022
text: district court by both the parties to a marriage together, whether such marriage was solemnised before or after the commencement of the , on the ground that they have been living separately for a period of one year or more, that they have not been able to live together and that they have mutually agreed that the marriage should be dissolved. 4 (2) On the motion of both the parties made not earlier than six months after the date of the presentation of the petition referred to in sub- section (1) and not later than eighteen months after the said date, if the period is not withdrawn in the meantime, the Court shall, on being satisfied, after hearing the parties and after making such inquiry as it thinks fit, that a marriage has been solemnised and that the averments in the petition are true, pass a decree of divorce declaring the marriage to be dissolved with effect from the date of the decree." 8. The Supreme Court in a case law reported in (2017) 7 SCC page 746 - has held that in the year 1976, the concept of divorce by mutual consent was introduced and however, section 13-B(2) contains

case_name: Sanjay Kumar Soni vs Smt. Geetanjali Chouhan on 28 September, 2022
text: the marriage to be dissolved with effect from the date of the decree." 8. The Supreme Court in a case law reported in (2017) 7 SCC page 746 - has held that in the year 1976, the concept of divorce by mutual consent was introduced and however, section 13-B(2) contains a bar to divorce being granted before six months of time elapsing after filing of the divorce petition by mutual consent. The said period was laid down to enable the parties to have a rethink so that the court grant divorce by mutual consent only if there is no chance for reconciliation. The Supreme Court has further laid down the following principles at para 19 : "19. Applying the above to the present situation, we are of the view that where the court dealing with a matter is satisfied that a case is made out to waive the statutory period under Section 13-B(2), it can do so after considering the following: (i) the statutory period of six months specified in Section 13-B(2), in addition to the statutory period of one year under Section 13-B(1) of separation of parties is already over before the first motion itself; (ii) all efforts for

case_name: Sanjay Kumar Soni vs Smt. Geetanjali Chouhan on 28 September, 2022
text: under Section 13-B(2), it can do so after considering the following: (i) the statutory period of six months specified in Section 13-B(2), in addition to the statutory period of one year under Section 13-B(1) of separation of parties is already over before the first motion itself; (ii) all efforts for mediation/conciliation including efforts in terms of Order 32-A / of the Act/ of the Family Courts Act to reunite the parties have failed and there is no likelihood of success in that direction by any further efforts; 5 (iii) the parties have genuinely settled their differences including alimony, custody of child or any other pending issues between the parties; (iv) the waiting period will only prolong their agony." 9. Applying the aforesaid principles to the present case, the facts would suggest that the parties are living apart since 2018 and both husband and wife have filed the application under of the Act of 1955 with joint signatures seeking mutual divorce as also to waive the cooling-off period of six months. Therefore, conduct of the parties and expressions made before the Court also shows that the parties cannot go together to continue with their matrimonial ties. 10. Since the wife has

case_name: Sanjay Kumar Soni vs Smt. Geetanjali Chouhan on 28 September, 2022
text: the Act of 1955 with joint signatures seeking mutual divorce as also to waive the cooling-off period of six months. Therefore, conduct of the parties and expressions made before the Court also shows that the parties cannot go together to continue with their matrimonial ties. 10. Since the wife has received the entire amount of Rs.6,31,500/- as permanent alimony, she stated that she does not want to continue further the case of domestic violence against the husband. Having stated so it further fortifies the intention of the parties that the marriage has completely come to an end for all practical purpose and there is no chance for reunion. Therefore, we deem it appropriate to waive the cooling off period of six months. Accordingly, I.A. No.02 of 2022 is allowed & stands disposed of. 11. Under the circumstances, we also allow the application for mutual divorce under of Act and deem it proper to grant a decree of divorce to the parties. Therefore, it is ordered that the marriage in between the parties on 15.04.2009 shall stand dissolved by a decree of divorce. 12. As per the settlement, the wife has received the amount of Rs.6,00,000/- as permanent alimony by way

case_name: Sanjay Kumar Soni vs Smt. Geetanjali Chouhan on 28 September, 2022
text: it proper to grant a decree of divorce to the parties. Therefore, it is ordered that the marriage in between the parties on 15.04.2009 shall stand dissolved by a decree of divorce. 12. As per the settlement, the wife has received the amount of Rs.6,00,000/- as permanent alimony by way of a demand draft before the Court and also received a further amount of arrears of maintenance, amounting to Rs.31,500/- which was due, so henceforth, she would not be entitled to any claim for maintenance. 6 13. As submitted by the wife before this Court that Miscellaneous Judicial Case No.18 of 2018, which is pending before the Judicial Magistrate First Class, Kharsiya under shall not be further prosecuted, therefore, she would also withdraw/get the application dismissed, which is pending as Miscellaneous Judicial Case No.18 of 2018 before the Judicial Magistrate First Class, Kharsiya. 14. In the result, the appeal is allowed. A decree be drawn accordingly. Sd/- Sd/- (Goutam Bhaduri) (Radhakishan Agrawal) Judge Judge Yogesh

case_name: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024
text: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024 Author: Bench: HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD ?Neutral Citation No. - 2024:AHC:132897-DB Court No. - 39 Case :- FIRST APPEAL DEFECTIVE No. - 403 of 2024 Appellant :- Shrey Sagar And Another Respondent :- Subhangi Sinha Counsel for Appellant :- Devesh Pratap Singh Chauhan,Saurabh Kumar Pandey Hon'ble Saumitra Dayal Singh,J. Hon'ble Donadi Ramesh,J. Civil Misc. Exemption Application No. Nil of 2024 1. Heard Sri Devesh Pratap Singh Chauhan learned counsel for the appellants. 2. Exemption has been sought with respect to requirement to file formal order. 3. Application is allowed. Exemption prayed for is granted. Order on Appeal 4. Heard Sri Devesh Pratap Singh Chauhan learned counsel for the appellants. 5. Present appeal has been filed under of the Family Courts Act, 1984 arising from the judgment and order dated 18.7.2024 passed by the Principal Judge Family Court, Gautambudh Nagar in Case No.662 of 2024, whereby the learned trial Court has rejected the application filed by the appellants seeking waiver of the cooling off period with respect to their petition filed under of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, seeking divorce through mutual consent. 6. Parties were married on

case_name: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024
text: Gautambudh Nagar in Case No.662 of 2024, whereby the learned trial Court has rejected the application filed by the appellants seeking waiver of the cooling off period with respect to their petition filed under of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, seeking divorce through mutual consent. 6. Parties were married on 09.12.2016. They are living separately since 23.7.2020. Almost after four years of separation suffered, they filed a petition seeking dissolution of their marriage, through mutual consent on 03.5.2024. That petition was supported by application seeking waiver of the cooling off period. It has been rejected. 7. As to other facts, it is undisputed between the parties that there are no children born to them and that mediation attempted has also failed. At present, both parties are described to be looking for second opportunity in life i.e. they are described to be looking to marry again. 8. Recently in ; 2023 8 ADJ 484, this Court had an occasion to consider the law in respect to waiver. It observed as under:- "4. The issue involved is no longer res integra. In (2017) 8 SCC 746, it was held as below : "19. Applying the above to the present situation, we are

case_name: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024
text: 8 ADJ 484, this Court had an occasion to consider the law in respect to waiver. It observed as under:- "4. The issue involved is no longer res integra. In (2017) 8 SCC 746, it was held as below : "19. Applying the above to the present situation, we are of the view that where the court dealing with a matter is satisfied that a case is made out to waive the statutory period under Section 13 B (2), it can do so after considering the following: (i) The statutory period of six months specified in B(2), in addition to the statutory period of one year under Section 13B(1) of separation of parties is already over before the first motion itself; (ii) All efforts for mediation/conciliation including efforts in terms of Order 32 A / of the Act/ of the Family Courts Act to reunite the parties have failed and there is no likelihood of success in that direction by any further efforts; (iii) The parties have genuinely settled their differences including alimony, custody of child or any other pending issues between the parties; (iv) The waiting period will only prolong their agony. The waiver application can be filed one

case_name: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024
text: there is no likelihood of success in that direction by any further efforts; (iii) The parties have genuinely settled their differences including alimony, custody of child or any other pending issues between the parties; (iv) The waiting period will only prolong their agony. The waiver application can be filed one week after the first motion giving reasons for the prayer for waiver. If the above conditions are satisfied, the waiver of the waiting period for the second motion will be in the discretion of the court concerned. 20. Since we are of the view that the period mentioned in B(2) is not mandatory but directory, it will be open to the court to exercise its discretion in the facts and circumstances of each case where there is no possibility of parties resuming cohabitation and there are chances of alternative rehabilitation." 5. That principle of law the Supreme Court has not been departed from, in any subsequent or other decision of that Court. 6. After considering the provisions of law, the Supreme Court had thus clarified that though the provision of Section 13B(2) postulates a cooling period of six months, that stipulation of time was not mandatory. Not only the provision

case_name: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024
text: been departed from, in any subsequent or other decision of that Court. 6. After considering the provisions of law, the Supreme Court had thus clarified that though the provision of Section 13B(2) postulates a cooling period of six months, that stipulation of time was not mandatory. Not only the provision stipulating six months' time period was held to be directory, further, the Supreme Court held that the discretion to waive that stipulation of time would vest in the Court dealing with the second motion petition. The intent of the decision of the Supreme Court and the interpretation of the law made by it are clear as daylight. Once the Supreme Court observed that the stipulation of time was directory and that the discretion to waive it may be exercised in the individual facts and circumstances of each case, by the Court, clearly, it had left no manner of doubt to arise with any party, less so the Court itself, as to who may exercise the discretion. 7. Insofar as the Supreme Court interpreted the statutory provision and laid down the law, that decision of the Supreme Court may never have been described as an exercise referable to of the Constitution

case_name: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024
text: arise with any party, less so the Court itself, as to who may exercise the discretion. 7. Insofar as the Supreme Court interpreted the statutory provision and laid down the law, that decision of the Supreme Court may never have been described as an exercise referable to of the Constitution of India. 8. of the Constitution of India enables the Supreme Court to do complete justice in the facts of any case. However, by very nature of that power, whenever exercised, that Court never seeks to lay down any proposition or principle of law. Here, to the contrary, the Supreme Court laid down the law in no uncertain terms. It took note of the statutory provision contained under of the Act, 1955 and interpreted it to reach the conclusion that the same was was directory and that the discretion to waive the stipulation of time would vest with the Court dealing with the second joint motion petition, seeking to dissolve the marriage between those parties." 9. Accordingly, we find that the ground for waiver is duly made out. The learned trial court has erred in overlooking that aspect of the matter. 10. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. 11. The period

case_name: Shrey Sagar And Another vs Subhangi Sinha on 20 August, 2024
text: dealing with the second joint motion petition, seeking to dissolve the marriage between those parties." 9. Accordingly, we find that the ground for waiver is duly made out. The learned trial court has erred in overlooking that aspect of the matter. 10. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. 11. The period of cooling off is waived. The second motion petition may be filed by the parties within a period of one week. It is expected that the learned trial court would act expediently and ensure that the proceedings are concluded as expeditiously as possible preferably within a period of two weeks, subject to due compliance and cooperation by the parties. Order Date :- 20.8.2024 Faraz (Donadi Ramesh, J.) (S. D. Singh, J.)

case_name: Smt. Monika vs Monu Gurjar on 20 January, 2025
text: Smt. Monika vs Monu Gurjar on 20 January, 2025 Author: Bench: NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-GWL:988 1 MP-196-2025 IN THE HIGH COURT OF MADHYA PRADESH AT GWALIOR BEFORE HON'BLE SHRI JUSTICE G. S. AHLUWALIA ON THE 20th OF JANUARY, 2025 MISC. PETITION No. 196 of 2025 SMT. MONIKA Versus MONU GURJAR Appearance: Shri Hemant Singh Rana - Advocate for petitioner. Shri Pallav Tripathi - Advocate for respondent. ORDER This petition under of the Constitution of India has been filed seeking the following reliefs:- "The order impugned Annexure - P/1 dated 23.12.2024 passed by Principal Judge, Family Court, Gwalior (M.P.) in RCSHM. No. 1498/2024 may kindly be set-aside and order or direction may kindly be issued commanding Principal Judge, Family Court, Gwalior (M.P.) to grant the divorce by mutual consent on the next date of hearing after relaxing the statutory period as prescribed under of Hindu Marriage Act. Any other relief which this Hon'ble Court deems fit in the facts and circumstances of the case may also kindly be granted." 2. It is submitted by counsel for petitioner that petitioner and respondent, who are legally wedded husband and wife, have filed an application under of Hindu Marriage Act for grant of divorce

case_name: Smt. Monika vs Monu Gurjar on 20 January, 2025
text: this Hon'ble Court deems fit in the facts and circumstances of the case may also kindly be granted." 2. It is submitted by counsel for petitioner that petitioner and respondent, who are legally wedded husband and wife, have filed an application under of Hindu Marriage Act for grant of divorce by mutual consent. They also filed an application for waiver of cooling off period of six months on the ground that the marriage has irretrievably broken down, however, by impugned order dated 23.12.2024 passed by the Principal Signature Not Verified Signed by: ANAND KUMAR Signing time: 21-Jan-25 7:37:23 PM NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-GWL:988 2 MP-196-2025 Judge, Family Court, Gwalior in Case No.1498-A/2023 (HMA) has rejected the said application. It is submitted by counsel for petitioner that the Supreme Court in the case of reported in (2017) 8 SCC 746 has held that the cooling off period is not mandatory but it is directory and accordingly it is submitted that the trial Court should have allowed the said application as the parties are residing separately from the month of March, 2023. 3. Considered the submissions made by the counsel for petitioner. 4. The Supreme Court in the case of (Supra) has

case_name: Smt. Monika vs Monu Gurjar on 20 January, 2025
text: it is directory and accordingly it is submitted that the trial Court should have allowed the said application as the parties are residing separately from the month of March, 2023. 3. Considered the submissions made by the counsel for petitioner. 4. The Supreme Court in the case of (Supra) has held that :- "16. The object of the provision is to enable the parties to dissolve a marriage by consent if the marriage has irretrievably broken down and to enable them to rehabilitate them as per available options. The amendment was inspired by the thought that forcible perpetuation of status of matrimony between unwilling partners did not serve any purpose. The object of the cooling off the period was to safeguard against a hurried decision if there was otherwise possibility of differences being reconciled. The object was not to perpetuate a purposeless marriage or to prolong the agony of the parties when there was no chance of reconciliation. Though every effort has to be made to save a marriage, if there are no chances of reunion and there are chances of fresh rehabilitation, the Court should not be powerless in enabling the parties to have a better option." 5 .

case_name: Smt. Monika vs Monu Gurjar on 20 January, 2025
text: parties when there was no chance of reconciliation. Though every effort has to be made to save a marriage, if there are no chances of reunion and there are chances of fresh rehabilitation, the Court should not be powerless in enabling the parties to have a better option." 5 . In the entire application, it is nowhere mentioned that the parties have decided to rehabilitate themselves. The only averment made in the application is that the marriage has irretrievably broken down and they are residing separately from 23.03.2023. The application for grant of divorce by mutual consent was filed sometimes in the month of October, 2024, therefore, even if the date of separation is treated as 23.03.2024, still it cannot be said that they are residing separately for the last several years Signature Not Verified Signed by: ANAND KUMAR Signing time: 21-Jan-25 7:37:23 PM NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-GWL:988 3 MP-196-2025 resulting in irretrievable breaking down of marriage. The purpose of cooling off period is to think over their decision to separate themselves. In absence of any averment that either of the parties have decided to rehabilitate themselves, then the very purpose of the law cannot be frustrated by casually waiving

case_name: Smt. Monika vs Monu Gurjar on 20 January, 2025
text: resulting in irretrievable breaking down of marriage. The purpose of cooling off period is to think over their decision to separate themselves. In absence of any averment that either of the parties have decided to rehabilitate themselves, then the very purpose of the law cannot be frustrated by casually waiving of cooling off period. 6. As no good ground was made out by petitioner as well as respondent for waiver of cooling off period, accordingly, no illegality was committed by the trial Court by rejecting the application for waiver of cooling off period. 7. Accordingly, the petition fails and is hereby dismissed. (G. S. AHLUWALIA) JUDGE AK/- Signature Not Verified Signed by: ANAND KUMAR Signing time: 21-Jan-25 7:37:23 PM

case_name: Xxxxxxxxxxxx vs Xxxxxxxxxxxx on 23 April, 2026
text: Xxxxxxxxxxxx vs Xxxxxxxxxxxx on 23 April, 2026 CR-3471-2026 1 130 IN THE HIGH COURT OF PUNJAB AND HARYANA AT CHANDIGARH CR-3471-2026 Date of Decision: 23.04.2026 SUKHDEEP SINGH GILL ...Petitioner Vs. KULDEEP KAUR GILL ...Respondent CORAM:- HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIRINDER AGGARWAL Present: Mr. B.S. Bhalla, Advocate for the petitioner. VIRINDER AGGARWAL, J. (Oral) 1. The present petition has been filed under of the Constitution of India seeking setting aside of the impugned order dated 10.03.2026 (Annexure P-2) passed by the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, Moga in case No. HMA/46 dated 31.01.2026, pending for 01.08.2026, whereby the application filed by the parties for waiver of the statutory cooling-off period of six months for recording of the second motion statement has been dismissed illegally and without proper application of mind. 2. Briefly stated, a petition under of the Hindu Marriage Act for dissolution of marriage by decree of divorce by mutual consent was filed jointly by the parties on the averments that their marriage was solemnized on 04.12.2010 according to Sikh rites and ceremonies and they cohabited as husband and wife but no issue was born, and since March 2022 the parties have been living separately, but, with the intervention of respectables

case_name: Xxxxxxxxxxxx vs Xxxxxxxxxxxx on 23 April, 2026
text: was filed jointly by the parties on the averments that their marriage was solemnized on 04.12.2010 according to Sikh rites and ceremonies and they cohabited as husband and wife but no issue was born, and since March 2022 the parties have been living separately, but, with the intervention of respectables and Panchayat, a settlement was arrived at whereby the respondent/wife had received her istridhan and both parties agreed to withdraw any cases filed against each other; that both parties, having mutually agreed that their marriage had irretrievably broken down, sought dissolution KUMUD VERMA 2026.04.24 15:55 I attest to the accuracy and integrity of this document Chandigarh CR-3471-2026 2 thereof by mutual consent without any fraud, coercion or misrepresentation. Respondent/wife, being residing in Canada, was represented through her lawful attorney, her father. After recording of the first motion statement, a joint application was filed by the parties seeking waiver of the statutory cooling-off period of six months for moving the second motion on the ground that the parties have been living separately since March 2022 with no possibility of reconciliation; however, the said application was dismissed by the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, Moga vide impugned order dated 10.03.2026 on the

case_name: Xxxxxxxxxxxx vs Xxxxxxxxxxxx on 23 April, 2026
text: statutory cooling-off period of six months for moving the second motion on the ground that the parties have been living separately since March 2022 with no possibility of reconciliation; however, the said application was dismissed by the learned Principal Judge, Family Court, Moga vide impugned order dated 10.03.2026 on the ground that no urgency was made out for waiver of the cooling-off period, which order is now under challenge in the present petition. 3. The learned Principal Judge, Family Court, Moga, vide order dated 10.03.2026, dismissed the joint application filed by the parties seeking waiver of the statutory cooling-off period of six months under of the Hindu Marriage Act for moving the second motion in a mutual consent divorce petition. The Court observed that although the parties had been living separately since March 2022 and their first motion statements were recorded on 31.01.2026, the statutory period under had not been specifically shown to be required to be waived on any urgent or exceptional grounds. The Court further held that mere assertion of irretrievable breakdown of marriage and the fact that one party was residing abroad were insufficient to establish urgency or rule out the possibility of reconciliation. 4. Accordingly, the

case_name: Xxxxxxxxxxxx vs Xxxxxxxxxxxx on 23 April, 2026
text: specifically shown to be required to be waived on any urgent or exceptional grounds. The Court further held that mere assertion of irretrievable breakdown of marriage and the fact that one party was residing abroad were insufficient to establish urgency or rule out the possibility of reconciliation. 4. Accordingly, the parties are granted 15 days' time from today to make sincere and bona fide efforts for reconciliation, so as to explore the possibility of an amicable settlement and preservation of their matrimonial relationship through the process of Court. The learned Presiding Officer, KUMUD VERMA 2026.04.24 15:55 I attest to the accuracy and integrity of this document Chandigarh CR-3471-2026 3 Family Court, shall facilitate the parties in this regard and respondent/wife Ms. Kuldeep Kaur Gill be allowed to participate through Video Conferencing. During the said period, the parties shall be at liberty to interact and make genuine efforts for resolving their disputes amicably, if so advised. It is further observed that in the event the reconciliation efforts fail and no settlement is arrived at between the parties within the aforesaid period, the learned Family Court shall thereafter consider, in accordance with law and on the basis of the facts and circumstances

case_name: Xxxxxxxxxxxx vs Xxxxxxxxxxxx on 23 April, 2026
text: disputes amicably, if so advised. It is further observed that in the event the reconciliation efforts fail and no settlement is arrived at between the parties within the aforesaid period, the learned Family Court shall thereafter consider, in accordance with law and on the basis of the facts and circumstances of the case, the question of waiver of the statutory cooling-off period of six months as prescribed under of the Hindu Marriage Act,1955. 5. Accordingly, the petition stands disposed of in terms of the aforesaid directions. (VIRINDER AGGARWAL) JUDGE 23.04.2026 kv Whether speaking/reasoned : Yes/No Whether reportable : Yes/No KUMUD VERMA 2026.04.24 15:55 I attest to the accuracy and integrity of this document Chandigarh

case_name: Shravan S/O Prabhakar Taywade And ... vs None on 14 November, 2025
text: Shravan S/O Prabhakar Taywade And ... vs None on 14 November, 2025 1 wp 6357.25.odt IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT BOMBAY NAGPUR BENCH : NAGPUR Writ Petition No.6357/2025 Shravan and another V None - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - ------------------------- Office Notes, Office Memoranda of Coram, Court's or Judge's orders appearances, Court's orders of directions and Registrar's orders - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

case_name: Shravan S/O Prabhakar Taywade And ... vs None on 14 November, 2025
text: - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - ---------------------------- Ms S.M. Tripathi, Advocate for petitioners. CORAM : PRAFULLA S. KHUBALKAR, J. DATE : 14-11-2025. Heard learned Counsel for petitioner nos.1 and 2. 2. It is submitted that since the challenge in the petition is to an order passed by the Family Court, rejecting the application filed by the petitioners for waiver of cooling off period in the petition seeking divorce by mutual consent, petitioner no.1 and petitioner no.2 are the only parties to the instant petition and nobody is arrayed as respondent. 3. Challenge raised by petitioner nos.1 and 2 is to an order dated 01-10-2025 passed by the Family Court, Nagpur, rejecting the application for waiver of statutory cooling off period. A perusal of the application shows that the parties have elaborately mentioned several reasons seeking waiver of

case_name: Shravan S/O Prabhakar Taywade And ... vs None on 14 November, 2025
text: arrayed as respondent. 3. Challenge raised by petitioner nos.1 and 2 is to an order dated 01-10-2025 passed by the Family Court, Nagpur, rejecting the application for waiver of statutory cooling off period. A perusal of the application shows that the parties have elaborately mentioned several reasons seeking waiver of cooling off period and by pointing out the relevant legal positions has prayed for waiver of cooling off period of six months under of the Hindu Marriage Act. However, on the day on which the application was filed the family Court has refused to entertain the application and rejected the application only on the count that the parties have not appeared before the Marriage Counsellor, which was a mandatory procedure in view of the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court. It is pointed out that only for this reason the application 2 wp 6357.25.odt is rejected. It is also submitted that the Family Court has not decided the application on merits even though the parties have made out a case for seeking waiver of cooling off period. 4. Learned Counsel for the petitioners has placed on record an affidavit dated 14-11-2025 sworn in by petitioner no.1 in which it is

case_name: Shravan S/O Prabhakar Taywade And ... vs None on 14 November, 2025
text: that the Family Court has not decided the application on merits even though the parties have made out a case for seeking waiver of cooling off period. 4. Learned Counsel for the petitioners has placed on record an affidavit dated 14-11-2025 sworn in by petitioner no.1 in which it is stated that on 01-10-2025 the parties had in fact appeared before the Marriage Counsellor when petitioner no.1 had appeared personally and petitioner no.2 wife had appeared virtually before the Marriage Councilor Advocate Mrs. Pande. This affidavit dated 14-11-2025 is tendered across the bar and it is taken on record. 5. Be that as it may, considering the fact that the parties have filed petition seeking divorce by mutual consent demonstrating the fact that no reconciliation is possible in between them and they have consciously decided to apply for divorce by mutual consent and further pointed out the circumstances in which the cooling off period was required to be waived, it is necessary in the interest of justice to allow the parties to appear before the Marriage Counsillor once again, physically if possible or virtually. 6. Considering the fact that the impugned order is passed only because the parties had failed

case_name: Shravan S/O Prabhakar Taywade And ... vs None on 14 November, 2025
text: the cooling off period was required to be waived, it is necessary in the interest of justice to allow the parties to appear before the Marriage Counsillor once again, physically if possible or virtually. 6. Considering the fact that the impugned order is passed only because the parties had failed to appear before the Marriage Counsellor for counselling, I think this is a fit case in which an opportunity needs to be granted to the parties to appear before the Marriage Counsellor on a fixed date and thereafter the Family Court is required to pass an order on the application seeking waiver of cooling off period. Considering the fact that the petition for divorce by mutual consent was filed on 01-10-2025, it 3 wp 6357.25.odt is directed that the parties should immediately take steps for appearing before the Marriage Counsellor if possible physically or virtually, by filing appropriate application within one week. 7. In view of these circumstances, impugned order dated 01-10-2025 passed by the Family Court on the application at Exhibit-07 is quashed and set aside. Matter is remanded to the Family Court for considering the application at Exhibit-07 afresh by allowing the parties to appear before the Marriage

case_name: Shravan S/O Prabhakar Taywade And ... vs None on 14 November, 2025
text: one week. 7. In view of these circumstances, impugned order dated 01-10-2025 passed by the Family Court on the application at Exhibit-07 is quashed and set aside. Matter is remanded to the Family Court for considering the application at Exhibit-07 afresh by allowing the parties to appear before the Marriage Counsellor and thereafter the Family Court is requested to pass orders on the application at Exhibit-07 within two weeks. 8. Writ Petition is accordingly disposed of. (Prafulla S. Khubalkar, J.) Deshmukh Signed by: Mr. S.Deshmukh Designation: PS To Honourable Judge Date: 15/11/2025 14:59:09

text: the following among other grounds. R S MANIDHAR PINGALI Counsel for the Respondent: 1. C INDRANI The Court made the following: 2 THE HON'BLE SRI JUSTICE BATTU DEVANAND & THE HONOURABLE SRI JUSTICE A. HARI HARANADHA SARMA FAMILY COURT APPEAL No.21 of 2024 JUDGMENT: (Per Hon'ble Sri Justice A. Hari Haranadha Sarma) Appellant is the husband and respondent is the wife. Appellant/husband appeared personally before the Court and is identified by his advocate, Ms. Y.S.Sowmya and respondent/wife appeared virtually and is identified by her counsel, Ms. C.Indrani. 2. Both parties submitted that they have entered into a settlement and a joint memo is also filed incorporating the terms of settlement. In the joint memo, both parties and their respective counsels have signed. Marriage between the parties took place on 08.03.2015 at Hyderabad. 3. It is represented that both parties have been living separately for the past seven (07) years.

case_name: Purohuth Naveen vs Purothit Mutha Krishna Pavani on 8 October, 2025
text: a joint memo is also filed incorporating the terms of settlement. In the joint memo, both parties and their respective counsels have signed. Marriage between the parties took place on 08.03.2015 at Hyderabad. 3. It is represented that both parties have been living separately for the past seven (07) years. There are no issues/offspring during the wedlock. All disputes between the parties have been settled and no litigations are pending. There are no other financial commitments inter se the parties. 4. Referring to joint memo, the counsel on record and the parties have requested for converting the present proceedings which were filed under 13 (1)(ia) (ib) into proceedings for divorce on mutual consent in terms of of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955. 3 5. Upon considering the length of separate living of the parties, the settlement of all disputes, their request for conversion of proceedings to divorce on mutual consent from contested one and also waiver of cooling off period, if any, are all found convincing. Accordingly, their request is accepted. 6. For the reasons stated above, the marriage between the parties dated 08.03.2015 is dissolved by way of a decree of divorce on mutual consent. The identification cards i.e.

case_name: Purohuth Naveen vs Purothit Mutha Krishna Pavani on 8 October, 2025
text: from contested one and also waiver of cooling off period, if any, are all found convincing. Accordingly, their request is accepted. 6. For the reasons stated above, the marriage between the parties dated 08.03.2015 is dissolved by way of a decree of divorce on mutual consent. The identification cards i.e. Aadhar Cards of the respective parties duly attested by learned counsel for parties and the joint memo with terms of settlement deed by parties shall form part of the record. 7. Accordingly, the Family Court Appeal is dismissed. There shall be no order as to costs. As a sequel, miscellaneous petitions pending, if any, shall stand closed. __________________________ JUSTICE BATTU DEVANAND __________________________________ JUSTICE A.
